sentences.text: By no means, no.
Our closest neighbor were non-Jews.
And they had a daughter my age, and we were actually very close friends, and we were in each other's homes frequently.
And my parents had neighbors, non-Jews.
So they were their-- they were very good neighbors.
No, we were not.
Los Angeles had three type of Jews.
Oh.
I-- I am sorry.
Of course Satu Mare.
There were the Orthodox [? image ?] we were part of.
I cannot tell you which segment was the larger one.
And then there were Status Quo.
I don't know.
I guess you would compare it to Reform.
And then there were Neolog.
No, pardon me.
The Status Quo would be like Conservative, and the Neolog would be like Reform.
But the Status Quo had also a beautiful synagogue, which was-- what shall I tell you-- the-- the real [INAUDIBLE] Jews call a synagogue kosher, the one which is segregated properly.
And the one which is not segregated properly is not such a kosher synagogue.
So this was also-- because the women were upstairs, but you could see downstairs.
It was a beautiful building, again, and the-- the chief rabbi was a doctor rabbiner.
He had a doctorate in philosophy, a very imposing, elegant man, very intelligent and highly educated and secularly also.
He was Dr.
Klein. ?] And then was the Reform.
I-- we had very little contact with that-- the other segments of the Jewish community.
I had some because I went to [ROMANIAN] with some of the-- Status Quo, and I was in some of their homes.
They were lovely people, but not observant like we were.
And it was-- they lived a very nice social life.
It was-- a lot among them, very well-to-do, with very elegant people, big businesses, big-name businesses.
No, they had their own.
They had their own.
This was the Orthodox, and they had their own.
Well, we were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how old?
The war-- well, of course I was aware of the war from-- I read newspapers from a very, very young age.
And we were always hoping-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a kind of hope that-- that the-- the-- the-- the Germans are going to-- are not going to succeed, you know?
They-- we are-- they are going to win, and everything is going to be fine.
Although it was, I guess, in 1942-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes.
In 1940-- let-- let me-- let me backtrack a little.
When I went to [ROMANIAN] and I was in fifth grade, I-- then already the Germans were taking over-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking over Czechoslovakia and Europe.
And of course, it reverberated in Romania also.

They-- they brought in some laws, which were not-- not existing before, that in the high schools-- in the [ROMANIAN], which was the state school, they didn't allow only a certain percentage of Jews-- 6% for a couple of years and a couple of year later none.
So of course, that touched my life already because I couldn't go on any further.
At this point, I-- I started school at seven, 11, I had to be about 14, 15.
Yes, yes.
So that disrupted my life.
I would have gone on to school, and I didn't know what to do.
But it disrupted-- but the whole Jewish life was disrupted, not only my life personally.
And-- but again, as I tell you, there were shortages.
There were-- there were terrible rumors.
There were trains going through the Satu Mare train station who brought Jews from Ukraine and from Russia who were already taken away from their homes.
But we thought that this is Russia.
This is communist.
And these Jews were not-- they-- they explained that-- that they were not really citizens.
They didn't have citizenship, and that's why they had to-- they were not safe.
So they-- but we said, it cannot happen to us.
I remember a Saturday afternoon when we walked to the train station, which was quite a walk, and we saw these cars filled with Jews, with Jews standing there.
They stopped there.
And we tried to-- we talked to them and tried to bring little things to help.
But it did not occur-- it seemed so far, this cannot happen to us.
We are-- we are-- we are not only citizens, but patriots.
My father was such a Hungarian patriot that it was unbelievable.
This-- this cannot happen with us.
Well, they started to take away certain businesses.
And then as time went on, they took away more and more.
Certain things what were-- what needed permission-- permits from the government, the-- the Jews were completely cut out.
And of course, then, two years later we wore the yellow star.
The yellow star we wore-- it was '44 when we were taken into the ghetto, so it was the year before.
No, no, no.
It was done by the Romanians, but it was influenced by the Germans.
And oh, pardon me, but in the mean-- while-- while I-- while I was still in-- in Satu Mare, Satu Mare became Hungary.
It reverted back to Hungary.
Romanians-- when I went to school, I did not feel antisemitism.
I knew that I cannot do certain things, but it did not occur to me that I should have wanted do it.
And as I told you, I had Romanian friends.
And-- and one very good thing was the Romania, you could do business.
For money, you could do everything.
So the Jews and the Romanians lived very well together.
It was very different in Hungary and-- and after the Hungarians came in.
Well, I was a young girl.
Of course, I remember when we started.
But how it happened exactly, it was-- it-- it was-- we were devastated.
We-- we couldn't go out only certain hours.
And-- but Satu Mare, the stars and the whole ghetto period were short.
Everything happened--
It was more resignation.
It was more resignation.
You know, we did not live in a ghetto, but the Jews grew up with a ghetto mentality, that you-- you-- you accept it, and you had to comply.
And that's-- that's how.
That was the-- the-- the-- the-- the philosophy.
That was the [? fate, ?] the mentality, that you don't-- revolution or something, uprising, I heard only in the-- in the Polish ghetto, you know?
No, it wasn't.
But again, I think that it's a fair explanation that they would-- we would have lived much longer under these circumstances, and-- and life would have been organized in some manner.
Because our staying in the ghetto was not organized at all.
It was a makeshift thing, you know?
And there was no time, because we were a few weeks-- four, six weeks-- and that's all.
So-- so if we would have lived much longer, then maybe something like that.
The whole thing was too short, and I guess we were-- we were in the shocking stage.
I don't remember actually how it was ordered.
I remember when we had to pack, and I still was a young girl.

We had to pack just minimal belongings, close the door with furniture, with everything, and taken into another part of the city what we-- we frequented rarely, where they-- they-- they kind of cordoned off a few blocks and-- and pushed Jews into, I don't know how many families, in one house.
Like, we lived in one room.
It-- it was-- but again, I tell you, it-- it's-- it's vague.
It's a mish-mash.
It's a mix up.
It's-- it's terrible.
It was--
Everybody had to enter the ghetto.
But there were three families in-- in Satu Mare who were exempt, who had-- whose parents had some very special-- how do you call when somebody has a medal--
Honors, awards from the Hungarian-- from the Austro-Hungarian government.
I don't know, military service or what.
And there, those three families were exempt.
When-- when I came back, I found them, and they were not deported.
Yes, in Satu Mare.
But otherwise, everybody.
But some people fled.
There were some people.
I know also some.
Even [NON-ENGLISH] Schwartz's sister, who, with-- with documents, which were not false documents, she fled.
And she went-- she fled to Romania, because in Romania there was no deportation, not at that time, at least.
So some fled, but those were rare individuals, you know.
A family that was-- I-- I don't think I heard a whole family fleeing.
Yes.
You would call it more of a Jewish part than we lived in, yes.
No, houses.
Apartments were only in the-- you know, big buildings with several stories were only in the center of the city.
Otherwise, it was private homes.
Of course.
They had to give over there.
All of them.
Only one brother who was in-- in-- in the labor.
And when-- when the Germans and the Hungarians came in, the Jews were not taken for military service, the Jewish young men.
They were taken in labor camps.
They worked behind the-- the front line doing cleaning up, you know, after bombing and after these kind of work.
Of course, there were adjacent area, different-- my husband, who lived about three hours away from [NON-ENGLISH], was taken into another town in a-- in-- in the ghetto.
Yes, I did, because--
No, he wasn't my husband.
Because, as I mentioned before, my grandfather had a mill.
And when he passed away, it was inherited by my uncle, by the oldest son.
And then he took in a partner who was my husband's father.
So I knew the family.
And he-- so the people from [NON-ENGLISH] were taken to [NON-ENGLISH].
That was a smaller town than Satu Mare.
But sure, there were many.
I-- I don't believe so.
I think it was only the Jews from Satu Mare.
There were rumors that we are going to be taken to-- to some places to work for the war effort.
Yes.
So-- but there were all kind of rumors going around.
We had no factual information.
So they started to empty the ghetto at the end of June, and we were taken out of the ghetto by transports.
Several-- several groups.
It-- we-- it was called transport, several groups.
And we were taken to the railway station.
We walked.
Now, let me tell you something very-- that I'll never forget.
It's in front of my eyes.
We were taken in groups, as I mentioned, and we were-- we were-- we were walking in the middle of the street.
Every traffic was stopped.
The street was practically empty.
The Gentiles were looking out through the closed window just behind the-- the-- the curtains out to the street.
And we were marched a row of five and, of course, escorted by Hungarian police with-- with arms.
And what-- and yes, let me backtrack again a little.
Before we were taken into the ghetto, the Jews were ordered to shave their beards.
The Orthodox [? form ?] Jews were wearing beards.
And my father had a beard, and he had to shave his beard.
Now, let me tell you.
My father, as I mentioned, was quite a few years older than my mother.
When I was born, I had already big brothers-- big-- big siblings from the first marriage.
So my-- I remember my father as an older man always with a beard.
And to see him without a beard-- he was gray already.
I will never forget that.
For days I was crying, and I couldn't look at him.
He was white.
He was wrinkled, you know, under the beard.
It was a trauma that-- it was the first-- that most terrible experience.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
--and he walking with his tears streaming down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will never forget that.
We reached the station.
It was quite a walk.
And we were taken into boxcars, and we were separated from my mother.
Because they took a certain amount of people until that boxcar filled up, and then the rest of the transport they put into another boxcar.
And that's what happened with us.
And that's when I saw my mother the last time, at the railway station in Satu Mare.
And my father didn't want to board the-- the-- the-- the train, and they pushed him to, because he was screaming, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], my mother.
And he sent me that I should look for her.
But I couldn't-- I couldn't find her.
So one sister and myself and my father went into another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] car, in the railway car, and the rest of the family were in the other.
With my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that, you know, I-- I didn't think of anything else, where we are going or what's going to happen.
I was-- I just thinking that I'm separated from my mother, and my father is crying there.
[INAUDIBLE].
The trip to Auschwitz, because that's where we were-- that's what the destination was-- of course, we didn't know where-- took two days and two nights.
So we arrived in Auschwitz the day after-- after [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
That's where we keep the yahrzeit, the first day after [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
For my parents and the rest of the family.
Well, so I told you it was a trip of two days--
--and two nights.
In these boxcars, little windows which had iron rods on it.
And I managed to get to the window, and there were peasants-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] peasants outside, you know, by the railway car.
And instead that they should give us something, I ask them, where are we going?
And they screamed back in Hungarian, to your death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took back-- I didn't tell anything to my father.
But I thought that's antisemitism, you know.
I didn't think that they know anything.
But they were vicious like-- So we arrived in Auschwitz.
Could you imagine?
Tired, broken, confused [INAUDIBLE].
Hungry.
And hungry, of course.
But-- but the other feelings, as it overtook the hunger, the-- the despair, the-- the-- the-- and I-- they took us-- we arrived in Zittau.
They opened the wagon.
We arrived in Auschwitz.
They opened the wagon, and they herded us out from the-- from the-- from the railway--
--car.
And there stood German soldiers with their arms and with dogs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we schlepped our bundles.
We were dressed in two, three-- three best dresses, hoping that-- that what we could take is going to remain.
And we were-- and they were rushing us, and we couldn't take all the bundles with us, and we were upset.
Who knew that what's coming that we wouldn't need the bundles.
But I still wanted to-- to look for my mother.
And I-- and I walked a few steps, and a soldier [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wrenched me back.
And so I didn't see my mother.
But they took away my father, too, because Mengele was there.
And suddenly we were just lined up, and we started to go march somewhere.
And somebody's there, an officer with-- a tall, good-looking man dressed as an officer with those high boots and with a-- he had like a riding-- how do you call it?
Crop.
Like a whip.
You know, a whip.
And he just moved the whip left and right, and my father was taken away.
We were-- we were pushed to one side, and my father was away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
Yes.
I was with one sister, and this is the only sister who remained-- she passed away two years ago in New York-- but who survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Would you like me to continue on Auschwitz?
Some were.
Some were.
Some were quite older, and some were younger.
I have no idea.
My younger sister was too young.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] An older sister passed away at home, pneumonia.
So--
Before the ghetto.
So my brothers-- I-- I didn't have other sisters.
I had a sister who passed away, a younger sister who was too young-- you know, the children were also annihilated like-- like the-- like the parent-- and a sister who survived and myself.
So we were four and five brothers.
Two brothers did not live in Satu Mare.
They were married.
They had their own families.
And the other three were taken.
I had-- I never had word of them.
Although no, somebody did come back and who said who was with one of my brothers.
And he kind of knew an approximate date of when he disappeared, of his death.
So that's the yahrzeit what we observe.
OK.
